question_title: Will NASA’s Northrop Grumman CRS-24 mission launch before May 1, 2026?
summary: Cygnus NG-24 is scheduled to launch NET April 8, 2026 on a SpaceX Falcon 9 from Cape Canaveral. The question asks whether it launches before May 1, 2026, giving 23 days of margin beyond the planned date. All indicators are positive: hardware has been at KSC since January 30, the ISS berthing port is clear (NG-23 departed March 12), no delays have been reported, and the Falcon 9 is an extremely reliable and high-cadence launcher. The main risks are tail events (Falcon 9 fleet stand-down, late spacecraft issue, ISS constraint), but even most of these would likely be resolved within the 23-day margin. I estimate ~95% probability of launching by May 1.

factors.description: Launch scheduled April 8, 2026 with 23 days margin to May 1 deadline - ample buffer for typical 1-2 week delays
Hardware ready: PCM shipped to KSC Jan 30, 2026 (2+ months integration time); service module assembled in Dulles, VA
ISS berthing port cleared: NG-23 departed March 12, 2026 and reentered March 14 - no port conflict
SpaceX Falcon 9 extremely reliable (98%+ success rate) with high cadence - quick turnaround for scrubs
No reported delays as of March 30, 2026 - all sources still show April 8 target date
Tail risk: Falcon 9 fleet-wide stand-down could cause multi-week delay (occurred ~2-3 times historically)
Tail risk: Late-discovered spacecraft integration issue (NG-22 cargo module damage precedent shows this can happen)
Tail risk: ISS operational constraint or scheduling conflict

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary: Will NASA's Northrop Grumman CRS-24 mission launch before May 1, 2026?

**Probability: 0.95 (95%)**

### Key Analysis

**Current Status:** Cygnus NG-24 is scheduled to launch **NET April 8, 2026** aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 from Cape Canaveral SLC-40. This gives **23 days of margin** before the May 1 deadline.

**Strong positive indicators:**
- 📦 **Hardware ready** — Pressurized Cargo Module shipped to KSC on January 30, 2026 (2+ months for integration)
- 🛰️ **ISS port clear** — NG-23 departed ISS March 12 and reentered March 14, freeing the Unity nadir berthing port
- 🚀 **Reliable launch vehicle** — Falcon 9 has 98%+ success rate and supports rapid turnaround from scrubs
- ✅ **No delays reported** — All sources (Wikipedia revised March 25, secondary payload teams) still reference April 8

**Risk factors (tail events):**
- Falcon 9 fleet stand-down (~5% probability, but even 2-week stand-downs would likely fit within margin)
- Late-discovered spacecraft issue (~3% probability of multi-week impact)
- ISS scheduling conflict (very unlikely given port is already clear)

**Bottom line:** Even if the April 8 date slips by 1-2 weeks (common for space missions), there's ample margin to still launch before May 1. Only a delay of 3+ weeks would threaten the deadline, which is uncommon when hardware is already at the launch site with no reported issues.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will NASA’s Northrop Grumman CRS-24 mission launch before May 1, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42925)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if NASA records CRS-24 as launched before May 1, 2026. It resolves to no otherwise. The primary source is NASA’s CRS-24 event page at https://www.nasa.gov/event/nasas-northrop-grumman-crs-24/ Question may also resolve according to  [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions)
fine_print: Use NASA’s own launch record or event page status. A scrub or attempt that does not achieve liftoff does not count. Candidate Sources: NASA news release on CRS-24 launch https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-invites-media-to-northrop-grumman-crs-24-station-resupply-launch/ ; NASA events page https://www.nasa.gov/events/

input.anchor: Base rate for ISS cargo missions launching within ~1 month of planned date when hardware is ready and no delays reported: ~90-95%. CRS missions on Falcon 9 have been quite reliable.
input.assessment:
This is a straightforward launch timing question with strong positive indicators. The mission is planned for April 8, 2026, giving 23 days of margin before the May 1 deadline. All hardware preparation is on track (PCM shipped 2 months ago), the ISS berthing port is clear (NG-23 departed March 12), and no delays have been reported. The launch vehicle is the extremely reliable SpaceX Falcon 9 with very high cadence.

The main risks are tail events: a Falcon 9 fleet-wide stand-down (which typically lasts 2-4 weeks but even then might still fit within the 23-day margin), a late-discovered spacecraft issue, or an ISS operational constraint. Even accounting for these, the probability of launching by May 1 is very high.

The 23-day margin is the key factor - even if the April 8 date slips by 1-2 weeks (common for space missions), there's still time. Only a delay of 3+ weeks would threaten the deadline, and such delays are uncommon when hardware is already at the launch site and integration is underway.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is ~92% (logit ~2.4). Factor analysis pushes slightly higher to ~95% given the specific strong indicators (no delays reported, hardware ready, port clear). The 23-day margin provides substantial buffer. Status quo check: if nothing changes, the mission launches on April 8 as planned = YES resolution.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether a Falcon 9 anomaly or stand-down could occur in the next month. 2. Whether any undisclosed technical issues exist with the spacecraft. 3. Whether ISS scheduling conflicts could emerge.
input.tool_audit: research() returned comprehensive data from Wikipedia, NASA, SpaceflightNow. fetch_url on NASA event page returned no archived snapshot. web_search confirmed April 8 planned date with no delay reports. No NASA.gov-specific results for CRS-24, but Wikipedia (last revised March 25, 2026) is reliable here. execute_code used for timeline and probability calculations.
input.update_triggers: A reported delay or reschedule of NG-24 would significantly change the forecast. A Falcon 9 anomaly/stand-down would be the most impactful negative event. Confirmation of final spacecraft integration or static fire would increase confidence.
input.process_reflection: The research tool worked well for this question - it returned comprehensive mission details from multiple sources. The NASA event page URL returned no content, which is a minor gap but Wikipedia and other sources filled in. The question is relatively straightforward for forecasting - a near-term launch with hardware already in preparation. The main challenge is quantifying tail risks for space launches.
